sentences.text: My mother, as I mentioned, was a very kind and very resourceful person.
She was always there to help other people.
And she managed to-- whoever was in trouble, so always called on my mother.
And I'm sure that she suffered terribly, the fact that she lost a daughter, and the circumstances under which she lost the daughter.
When I look at it from a distance, that wasn't really only compensation for something.
I'm sure that she was like that always.
The only thing she tried to persuade me, and tried to push a little bit, was me playing the violin.
I somehow liked music, but to train for hours was against somehow my principle of freedom.
It was much easier to play hockey, or to play hooky or something similar.
But she had a gentle way of persuasion.
And my father bought me a very beautiful instrument, which was an Italian instrument.
It wasn't a Stradivarius, or something similar.
But it was from the end of the 18th century.
I couldn't appreciate what's going on-- only when I lost-- when I had to give it away, then I realized.
And I was pretty good already at that point.
I-- when Hitler marched in, I was capable to prepare for [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] beginning of the Brahms concerto.
But that was, my mother who was pushing.
Then, when things got really tough, so-- she managed always to help people on the other side of the city, which was forbidden.
And she was willing to take off her-- the Star of David, and was willing simply to go and save somebody or bring something for somebody.
And finally when we were deported, I went with a different transporter.
When she arrived on the spot, I tried always to find something for her to shield her, the same way in Auschwitz.
But I don't think that she would need it.
She was pretty on her own.
The fact that she came so late, after the war, was that she was-- she escaped from a death march, and was found by the Russians, who took her to the commanding officer, who took some liking in her.
And she became a cook for the division.
And then he became enchanted with her and didn't want to let her go.
So she literally had to escape from the Russians where she was-- they were, at that point, somewhere around the outskirts of Berlin.
I have to correct you.
There was no Jewish school.
No.
On my father's side, it was-- father had to make some kind of an exception when he married my mother.
For father's family was, actually, far more religiously involved than we were.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] My uncle, Heinrich-- H-E-I-N-R-I-C-H-- was a very practicing Jew.
And my relatives who lived-- my two aunts who lived in Germany, they were practicing Jews.
My uncle Heinrich didn't have any children, so I was somehow the favorite son at that point.
On my mother's side, it was quite a different story.
This little place where my grandfather was-- was-- lived and was born, and my mother was born, they were the only Jews in the village.
And in the vicinity of the village, there were other little towns and villages.
There might have been, I don't know, two dozen Jewish-- two dozen Jewish families living in a radius of about 25 kilometers.
And they had this little shul, and they had this little cemetery.
Already, my grandfather wasn't practicing too much.
My grandfather, I remember, kept the high holidays, went to this little shul of this little-- one called-- can call it synagogue, it was too small for it, but still.
And I remember that he kept Passover.
Passover, he kept for, one point, for one ate matzohs.
And they brought-- a truck brought matzohs to the Jews in the local region.
And they were round, in parcels of about, I would say, 1 foot high.
And the whole village, all the farmers, all the peasants were waiting for the matzohs.
For he brought the matzohs not only for himself, but every family had to get matzohs.
It became some kind of a special feast towards a more special honor towards him.
As I mentioned, I never met a speck of anti-Semitism in that village.
And that made it very difficult, for my grandfather was primarily a Czech.
My grandmother was Czech.
My mother was Czech.
The Jewish, it was just incidental religion.
There was absolutely no Jewish tradition in it.
You know, and people start to talk about tradition.
Tradition was gone at that point in time.
The same way with father's side.
They wanted to be German-- they wanted to be German more than being Jewish.
So they became, I mean, what I then later find out was specific for the German Jews, [NON-ENGLISH], that-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the kind of nationalism took over the religion.
Obviously, it didn't pay.
Now, we kept high holidays.
We kept the Rosh Hashanah, we kept the Yom Kippur.
And I remember that, at my uncle's, I had something like Hanukkah, occasionally-- not always.
My grandfather would go so far that to comply with the village there was a-- a Christmas tree there.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] This is not just some presents which were given-- and mentioned that it coincides with Hanukkah.
And there was a menorah in my grandfather's house, but that was about all.
But it was always something which coincides with the goyish or the Aryan type of surrounding.
I went to the synagogue with my father and with my mother.
And it was already so progressive that there were men and women together, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] which probably, for that time, was very reformed.
The rabbi was a very nice guy, a very nice man with a beard.
And he was very kind.
I had a bar mitzvah.
I was prepared for it by, I forgot the name of the melamed who taught me.
And I learned it by heart.
I didn't know what I am saying.
A prayer so similar, my grandfather invoked prayers, especially in the village, which was in the mountains, and that was a storm coming.
And there were the lightnings coming down, and simply there was the thunder.
And everything was trembling.
Suddenly, there was a Bible on the table.
And my grandfather was praying.
So I connected always the prayer to some kind of a fear-- why one prays.
And I remember that the bar mitzvah-- we had at home some kind of a family reunion.
My relatives and some friends, I don't know how many people were there.
But it was done at home, simply at a dining room table, and the rabbi was there.
In school, religion was taught as a subject.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] It was either a Roman Catholic religion, or the Protestant.
Or the Jews, they had their religion classes were fused.
Usually in the afternoon, one hour or two hours in the afternoon.
And we had a teacher-- funnily enough, Eva had the same teacher as I had.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the only thing what I remember is a little bit being taught the Bible more or less as a historical document, and some basic prayers.
We were singing something, the melodies I remembered.
And we were torturing the poor man, I mean, to destruction.
That I remember.
It was called Savazska.
S-A-V-A-Z-S-K-A synagogue-- a synagogue.
It was a relatively nice building, built, I think, the turn of the century.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] it was attended, I would say by-- the congregation might have had, maybe I am exaggerating, but between 500 or 1,000 people.
And I remember simply before one went into the synagogue, walking through the street and the-- a non-Jewish population simply watching the Jews walking in their fineries.
It was about-- hm-- five blocks, six blocks from the place I lived.
It was in Prague.
At that point, it was Prague 12-- very close to this square where I-- where I went to my grammar school.
His name was Rabbi Sicher, S-I-C-H-E-R.
And he succeeded to leave [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] before the Nazi calamity, for Palestine.
And he returned after the war to Prague.
He was reinstated in some-- I think in a different function, for the Jewish population was decimated at that point.
And he died in Prague, and he's buried in Prague.
The synagogue, I think, was changed into a school-- which, somehow, I don't mind, for it, at least, fulfills some purpose.
The Nazis did not destroy any of the Prague synagogues.
They used them for various purposes, usually as magazines or-- or storage.
But they were standing after the war.
Also, downtown, in the old Jewish section-- which is, I mean, nowadays the center of the attention of anybody who is a visitor to Prague-- is the old Jewish section.
And there are literally four synagogues within the radius of 200 meters.
One of them is from the 12th century, a very stern building.
And one of them is the synagogue where there are the names of all the deceased.
There are about 90,000 names on the walls of all the Jews from the Czech and Moravian lands who died during the Holocaust.
And then one of them was changed to a museum.
And in that is wedged an old Jewish cemetery.
That is from 14th, 15th century.
And there is lying the famous Rabbi Low, L-O-W, who made the miracle of creating a golem.
And this was kept by the Nazis, under the Nazis.
They didn't touch it.
They left there, even, a clock with Jewish lettering.
It-- the clock goes anti-clockwise.
And they were gathering all of the kinds of religious objects from all the lands, and brought it to Prague and threw it into one of the synagogues-- all the books or all the Torahs.
For they wanted, after the war, to show the world that it is not so bad, that they are-- have got still a kind of human philanthropic trend.
The wanted to create there a museum of extinct people, using this part, this section, of the city for it.
I'm 16 years old at that point.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The kind of restrictions which, already, the Germans, the Nazis, tested in Germany and in Austria-- obviously which was occupied exactly one year prior to that.
So they implied all the laws and bylaws concerning Jews, literally within 24 hours.
The border became open until the end of March.
If somebody had, for instance, a passport, he could leave for 14 days.
At that point, simply, they stopped all the traffic.
For the Jews, they simply seized-- within 24 hours-- they seized all the Jewish accounts, anything what has got-- what had to do something with money, or with any kind of valuables.
They blocked all the safes, all the deposit boxes.
And within literally two months, they released all the public employees who were Jews-- all the professors or teachers at schools, at universities, at public offices.

They-- literally, every day-- every day, I may be exaggerating-- but every week, there was a different kind of ordinance or different kind of bylaw, different kinds of order issued to the Jewish community, which then had the duty to spread it among its members, or people who were considered for Jewish, limiting, I mean, anything one can imagine.
In other words, we were not permitted to use the trains.
We are not permitted to leave Prague.
We are not permitted to move in certain parts of the city.
Eventually, we were given the curfew of 8 o'clock.
We were not permitted to buy things in any store, with exception of stores which were designed-- designated for Jews.
And only a certain food was given to Jews, and in certain quantity on certain days.
The Jews couldn't buy anything connected to art, to music.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They--
My life changed simply that I felt that I made a catastrophic mistake that I didn't leave.
And I couldn't imagine what will happen.
I somehow had the feeling that something extremely odd is happening, and very sinister is happening.
If one read the newspaper, which was by that point [GERMAN], meaning everything along the line of the party-- so the Czech newspaper became, I mean, just the mouthpiece of the German newspapers.
And the Jews were the favorite target of any misery or anything, whatever that went wrong.
And the Jews became the same type of theoretically vermin-- as in the German nomenclature.

Then we had to give away-- which, at that point, didn't mean anything to me, with one little exception-- anything from precious metals, anything which would be antique, any kind of fur, any kind of heavy coat, any kind of--
Everything.
Everything what I just mentioned.
I gave away stamp collection, and you can-- if you had any kind of silver, gold, cutlery, I mean, more valuable china-- which was specifically named.
And finally, I had to give away my violin, which really hit me badly.
There was gathering places, simply, where you had to bring it to this gathering place, and you got a specific kind of list what you gave there.
And if you wouldn't, so the penalty was quite clear cut.
Simply, the penalty was imprisonment, and probably concentration camp.
Interestingly enough, the Czech population looked aside.
The Czech population-- the great majority simply didn't denounce anyone.
They were-- to be denounced by a Czech, it was an absolute rarity.
There was naturally a small segment of Czech fascists.
But it was a real exception.
Like, I met-- the first time, really, when I felt that something is going really wrong, I met one former classmate who actually failed from the gymnasium.
His name was Bozanovak, B-O-Z-A-N-O-V-A-K.
We had already stars at that point.
We got-- the order came to have Stars of David, with the Jude on it, Jew.
And I was walking, just in front of my block, and I met Boza, who spat on me and ordered me off the sidewalk.
It was a kind of a shattering experience for me.
The newspaper, the kind of pressure, now this.
That evening, I persuaded my father to come with me, and I joined the Zionist movement.
It was down in downtown Prague.
It was, again, originally part of the Jewish section.
The Prague Ghetto was dismantled in the end of 17th century.
But that was part-- originally part of the ghetto.
And this is where I met a kind of a very good looking, exceptionally good looking young woman.
Mm?
Oh, no, no, no, I don't know.
I think it was quite honest what I said.
I joined the movement for-- I knew from a friend of mine, by the name of P-A-C-H-N-E-R, who joined the movement, and he left with an illegal transport to Palestine.
It was in 19-- at that point in 1939, 1940.
And I wanted to leave.
I said to myself, this is a way how to leave.
The movement at that point was called El Al.
And the section was Tekhelet Lavan.
El Al would be high up.
And Tekhelet Lavan is blue and white-- ah, white, sorry, blue and white, correct.
And I was attached to a small group of people.
We had been about 14, called the [NON-ENGLISH], the group.
And we had a [NON-ENGLISH], her name was Eva, Eva Hahn, H-A-H-N-- she is still alive, and lives in Israel.
And [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] we all went to it with a vengeance.
For we all suddenly had these bad experiences with being Czech.
Suddenly we found out we are not Czechs, we are Jews.
And according to the Nuremberg laws, laws of the city of Nuremberg in 1935, a Jew was somebody whose grandparents were Jewish-- whether he was baptized or not, there was no exception.
So that in the [NON-ENGLISH] were some-- especially two girls who were Roman Catholics, and suddenly they were-- they turned Zionist, they turned Jewish.
And we started to learn Hebrew with a tremendous speed.
There was a schaliach there from Israel-- from Palestine, sorry.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] His name was Tzvi, forgot the last name.
And he was teaching Hebrew.
He was teaching [HEBREW].
And he took me as a kind of private project, so that I was studying Hebrew for about five-- four or five hours a day.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we were being taught what's happening in Palestine.
And we were singing Hebrew songs.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the rest of my Hebrew, which is still within me-- it depends on the songs.
I still know about, I would say, minimum 50 of Hebrew songs, and I can translate them.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I-- as I mentioned, I met Eva, and we fell in love, being very young.
And we became very courageous-- probably very stupid, also.
Like we both had our stars on just hooks.

And we unhooked the star and went together to a movie, which was forbidden, or to the part of the city which was forbidden, or took a streetcar to the edge of the city and went through the woods-- which, naturally, was forbidden.
But we were not caught.
Our usual place where we met, and when we simply dated, was the old Jewish cemetery, where we simply were alone.
And it was a nice solitude, sitting on the grave of the great Rabbi Low.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] She was working-- she was attending, for another year, when she was-- had to leave the school, in a private kind of school.
And she taught-- was taking English and French.
And she tried to teach me a little bit.
OK.
